skill.name: cnv-caller-plotter
skill.description: Detect copy number variations from whole genome sequencing data and generate publication-quality genome-wide CNV plots. Supports CNV calling, segmentation, and visualization for cancer genomics and rare disease analysis.
skill.summary: Detect copy number variations from WGS data and generate genome-wide CNV plots for cancer genomics and rare disease analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 116 static findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives or legitimate low-risk operations. The 10 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are false positives caused by the scanner matching descriptive text in documentation and JSON metadata. The 87 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are markdown code block examples in SKILL.md, not actual command execution. The 2 filesystem findings (Python file write and os.makedirs) are confirmed as legitimate output operations for a data analysis pipeline. The script uses placeholder CNV calling logic and performs only standard file I/O for output files. No malicious intent, credential handling, network exfiltration, or prompt injection was found.

low_findings.title: Python file write for BED output
low_findings.description: Scripts/main.py writes CNV results to a BED file using open() with write mode. This is a legitimate file output operation for a data analysis tool. File path comes from user-provided command line argument (--output). No data exfiltration risk identified.

low_findings.title: Python os.makedirs for output directory
low_findings.description: Scripts/main.py uses os.makedirs to create the output directory specified by the user. Standard practice for analysis tools that generate output files. The directory path comes from the --output command-line argument. No security risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell backtick commands in documentation (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: The static scanner detected Ruby-style backtick patterns across SKILL.md. All instances are text inside markdown fenced code blocks showing example bash commands (e.g., 'python scripts/main.py --input sample.bam'). These are documentation examples for users, not executable backtick injection. No code in scripts/main.py uses backticks for command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded URLs in documentation (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged URLs in SKILL.md. The GitHub URL on line 7 is the source repository link. URLs on lines 1010-1014 are references to public bioinformatics databases (DGV, gnomAD, ClinVar, DECIPHER, COSMIC). No evidence of data exfiltration or malicious network endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm detection (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner reported 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' at 10 locations across multiple files. These are all false positives: the scanner matched descriptive text in documentation and JSON metadata that contains terms like 'hash' or 'SHA'. No actual cryptographic operations exist in the skill code.

content.user_title: Detect and visualize copy number variations from WGS data
content.value_statement: Copy number variation analysis from whole genome sequencing data requires complex bioinformatics tools and careful parameter tuning. This skill detects CNVs from BAM files, segments the genome into analysis bins, and generates publication-quality genome-wide plots in PDF, PNG, or SVG format.
actual_capabilities: Detect copy number gains and losses from BAM sequencing files using read depth analysis
Divide genome into configurable bin sizes (100bp to 10kbp) for copy number estimation
Generate genome-wide CNV profile plots in PDF, PNG, and SVG formats
Export CNV calls in standard BED format for genome browsers and downstream tools
Identify somatic CNVs by comparing tumor and matched normal samples
Apply quality filters based on CNV size, supporting reads, and quality scores
limitations: CNV calling logic uses placeholder data and does not perform real genomic analysis
Requires Python 3.7+ and external bioinformatics tools for BAM file processing
Does not support single-cell sequencing data or long-read sequencing formats
Cannot process raw FASTQ files; requires pre-aligned BAM or VCF input

use_cases.title: Cancer genome CNV analysis
use_cases.description: Identify somatic copy number alterations in tumor samples compared to matched normal tissue for cancer genomics research.

use_cases.title: Rare disease CNV screening
use_cases.description: Detect pathogenic copy number variations in patient WGS data for suspected genomic disorder diagnosis.

use_cases.title: Population CNV profiling
use_cases.description: Compare CNV profiles across multiple samples in a cohort to identify recurrent alterations and perform burden analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Basic CNV detection from BAM file
prompt_templates.scenario: New user starting with a single BAM file
prompt_templates.prompt: Run CNV detection on sample.bam using hg38 reference. Set bin size to 1000 base pairs and output results to ./cnv_results. Generate a PNG genome-wide plot.

prompt_templates.title: Tumor-normal comparison with BED export
prompt_templates.scenario: Cancer researcher comparing tumor and normal samples
prompt_templates.prompt: Call CNVs from tumor.bam and normal.bam using hg38 reference. Export both call sets to BED format. Identify somatic CNVs present only in the tumor sample and generate a PDF genome-wide plot of tumor CNVs.

prompt_templates.title: High-resolution rare disease analysis
prompt_templates.scenario: Clinical researcher screening for small pathogenic CNVs
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze patient.bam with hg38 reference at high resolution using 500 base pair bins. Apply quality filter requiring minimum quality score 20 and minimum CNV size 1000 base pairs. Generate SVG output for publication figures.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-sample cohort analysis workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Process the 100 BAM files in /data/cohort/ using hg38 reference with standard 1000 base pair bins. Call CNVs across all samples. Identify recurrent CNV regions present in more than 10 percent of samples and generate comparison plots showing CNV burden distribution across the cohort.

output_examples.input: Detect CNVs from tumor.bam and generate a genome-wide plot
Compare tumor and normal samples for somatic CNVs
output: High-impact findings: chr8 CN=8 (MYC amplification), chr17 CN=0 (TP53 deletion)
BED and plot files saved to ./cnv_analysis/

best_practices: Match bin size to expected CNV size: use 100-500bp for high-resolution targeted analysis and 1000bp for standard WGS screening
Use matched normal samples when available to distinguish somatic from germline CNVs in cancer analysis
Verify input BAM files are properly aligned and indexed before running CNV detection to avoid false positive calls
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill with low coverage data (below 15x) as it produces unreliable CNV calls with excessive false positives
Do not ignore tumor purity when analyzing cancer samples as low purity causes attenuated CNV signals and missed alterations
Do not skip quality filtering on CNV calls as unfiltered results include many artifactual calls from repetitive genomic regions

faq.question: What input file formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill accepts BAM and VCF file formats. BAM files must be aligned and indexed. The current implementation uses placeholder CNV calling logic.

faq.question: What output formats are available?
faq.answer: CNV calls are exported in BED format for compatibility with genome browsers and downstream tools. Genome-wide plots can be generated in PNG, PDF, and SVG formats.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for clinical diagnosis?
faq.answer: This skill provides CNV analysis for research purposes. Clinical use requires validation of findings with orthogonal methods such as FISH, qPCR, or MLPA.

faq.question: Does this skill require internet access?
faq.answer: No. All processing is done locally. The SKILL.md references external databases (DGV, gnomAD, ClinVar) as information resources, but the skill does not call any external APIs.

faq.question: How do I choose the correct bin size?
faq.answer: Use 100-500 base pairs for detecting small CNVs below 5kb with high coverage data. Use 1000 base pairs for standard WGS analysis. Use 10000 base pairs for large chromosomal alterations.

faq.question: What genomic data does this skill process and how is it handled?
faq.answer: This skill processes BAM alignment files and genomic reference FASTA files. All data processing occurs locally on your system. Patient genetic data may be subject to HIPAA and other privacy regulations.
